expert_claims: C1: A canonical JSON envelope (RFC 8785 JCS, sorted-keys) is needed across the 6 SOLL-serving read tools.
C2: render_hash excluding volatiles enables a 'replay byte-identical on 100 queries' regression test that locks output stability.
C3: A bytes budget (12K default / 80K max) + truncation_reason enum gives the LLM deterministic, bounded payloads.
C4: adjacent_context (parents<=5 / children<=10 by density DESC, conflicts, superseded_by) materially improves the LLM's structural awareness per call.
C5: rules_in_effect[] cascaded with scope_predicate discriminated union surfaces the binding governance the LLM must obey at each node.
C6: markdown_render as a MODE param (render:json|json+markdown) instead of a separate tool reduces surface and unifies the dual-channel.
C7: mode=lean|rich escape + suggested_next_tool (max 3) tune verbosity and routing per call.
C8: lifecycle.decay_score snapshot in the envelope (decay_snapshot_at) helps the LLM weight stale intent.
C9: schema_version_requested/served + density_score deterministic formula (GUI-AXO-1024) make the envelope evolvable and reproducible.

premise_assessment.claim: C1 canonical sorted-keys JSON envelope across 6 tools
premise_assessment.reasoning: Partly already true: serde_json is compiled WITHOUT preserve_order, so Value::Object is BTreeMap-backed and keys ALREADY serialize sorted. Full RFC 8785 JCS adds only number-normalization + Unicode-escape rules on top — a small delta, not a from-scratch build. But the envelope must wrap 6 tools whose canonical shape is already {content:[markdown], data:json}; introducing a THIRD envelope semantics risks colliding with the two existing band/budget envelopes (REQ-AXO-264 retrieve_context_layered, REQ-AXO-91524 tri-modal). Need is real but partially met; the marginal canonicalization value is lower than the spec implies.

premise_assessment.claim: C2 render_hash + byte-identical replay test
premise_assessment.reasoning: Feasible but fragile. Empirically the responses are riddled with volatiles (elapsed_ms, cache now_ms, git --since=24h recent_band). Good news: the sort comparators at tools_context.rs:1510/1837 already carry deterministic tiebreakers (uri, id), so within ONE frozen IST snapshot ordering is stable. Byte-identical replay therefore requires (a) strict volatile stripping AND (b) a pinned snapshot AND (c) excluding/mocking the git-time-window band. The test is achievable but its pass/fail hinges on engineering rigor that the spec underestimates; the recent_band is genuinely non-reproducible without a clock/git fixture.

premise_assessment.claim: C3 bytes budget + truncation_reason
premise_assessment.reasoning: A token/budget/truncation_reason envelope ALREADY ships for the highest-value tool (retrieve_context_layered: tokens_used/tokens_budget/tokens_overflowed, REQ-AXO-264 A3 v2). format.rs already truncates with a [truncated=true, mode=brief, max_chars=4000] marker. Slice 3 re-specifies this in BYTES instead of TOKENS and generalizes to inspect/why/path. Net-new value is the generalization, but there is real duplication risk: two budget vocabularies (tokens vs bytes) on adjacent tools confuses the LLM more than it helps.

premise_assessment.claim: C4 adjacent_context by density DESC
premise_assessment.reasoning: Cannot be built in Slice 3 as specified. 'by density DESC' depends on density_score (GUI-AXO-1024 deterministic formula) and on Layer A metadata jsonb — both GREENFIELD (Slices 2/4, not implemented). The current SOLL surface has REFINES edges (tools_soll.rs:50) but no density signal and no parents/children adjacency tool. The structural-awareness value is real, but the substrate it reads does not exist yet, so this claim is premature for Slice 3 and would ship a hardcoded/placeholder density (the same anti-pattern as snapshot_id:null in the existing layered envelope).

premise_assessment.claim: C5 rules_in_effect[] cascaded with scope_predicate
premise_assessment.reasoning: Same substrate gap as C4. scope_predicate is a discriminated union {always|entity_type|tag|phase|custom} defined in Layer A metadata (DEC-AXO-901623) which is not in the schema. No scope_predicate / inheritance{binding|suggested|reference} field exists anywhere in src/. Cascading rules requires Layer A inheritance metadata first. High potential value (this is arguably the single most useful envelope field for an agent), but blocked on Layer A — building it now means inventing the cascade against an absent data model.

premise_assessment.claim: C6 markdown as MODE param not separate tool
premise_assessment.reasoning: Empirically sound and low-cost. The dual-channel {content:[{type:text,text:<markdown>}], data:{json}} ALREADY exists on every target tool, and a mode param (brief/full/verbose) already exists (format.rs evidence_by_mode, tools used at tools_context.rs:587/1286/1747). Folding render:json|json+markdown into the existing mode plumbing avoids a new tool — directly aligned with the consolidation thesis. This is the cleanest, most defensible part of the slice and reduces surface.

premise_assessment.claim: C7 mode=lean|rich + suggested_next_tool max 3
premise_assessment.reasoning: Marginal. lean/rich is a verbosity knob layered on the existing brief/full/verbose mode — redundant unless brief/full are retired in the same change (otherwise the LLM faces TWO overlapping verbosity vocabularies). suggested_next_tool is genuinely useful for routing (helps the deferred-tool harness pick the next ToolSearch target) but is independent of the envelope and could ship standalone. Value is real but small and partly redundant with existing mode.

premise_assessment.claim: C8 lifecycle.decay_score snapshot in envelope
premise_assessment.reasoning: decay_score is server-computed Layer A metadata (DEC-AXO-901623) — greenfield, no field in src/. Cannot snapshot a value that is not computed. Blocked on Layer A lifecycle work. Premature for Slice 3.

premise_assessment.claim: C9 schema_version + density formula reproducibility
premise_assessment.reasoning: schema_version_requested/served + dual-read window is sound engineering for an evolvable contract, but it only earns its keep AFTER the schema exists and starts evolving — building version negotiation before v1 of the schema ships is premature optimization. density_score formula (GUI-AXO-1024) is invalidated by the same Layer A gap as C4/C5. The versioning scaffolding is defensible to design now but not to fully wire.

eval.experiment_needed: Run an in-tree harness that calls the 6 target tools twice against a PINNED dev IST snapshot (indexer stopped, brain serving frozen) on a fixed query set of ~20, strip the known volatiles (elapsed_ms, cache now_ms, git recent_band), and diff the two runs byte-for-byte. This empirically measures (a) what fraction of output is ALREADY deterministic vs what render_hash must exclude, and (b) whether the recent_band/git-time-window is the only true non-determinism source. If >=18/20 already replay-identical after stripping the 3 known volatiles, the render_hash claim (C2) is cheap and the byte-identical test is real — push build_confidence toward 70. Separately, prototype adjacent_context/rules_in_effect against the CURRENT schema to confirm empirically they cannot be populated without Layer A (expected: they return empty/placeholder, confirming the substrate gap that invalidates C4/C5/C8).
risks: Substrate inversion: 4 of 9 envelope fields (adjacent_context, rules_in_effect, decay_score, density_score) are INVALIDATED because Layer A metadata (Slices 2/4) is greenfield. Building Slice 3 as specified ships placeholder/null fields (repeating the existing snapshot_id:null anti-pattern in retrieve_context_layered) — a contract that promises governance/structural data it cannot deliver.

Third-envelope collision: two budget/band/truncation envelopes already ship (REQ-AXO-264 token bands, REQ-AXO-91524 tri-modal). A bytes-based Layer B envelope on adjacent tools gives the LLM TWO budget vocabularies (tokens vs bytes) and THREE envelope shapes — net surface confusion, the opposite of the MIL-27 consolidation thesis.

High blast-radius on in-flight read tools: inspect/retrieve_context/retrieve_context_v2/why/path/soll_query_context are the tools the agent uses most in every session (Phase A onboarding, code diagnosis). Changing their output shape risks regressing the dual-channel {content,data} contract that downstream parsing (and the deferred-tool harness) depends on. Must ship behind a render param default-off, not a global shape change.
Byte-identical replay test is brittle vs the git --since=24h recent_band: without a clock/git fixture the test flakes, eroding trust in the regression gate it is meant to provide.
Verbosity-vocabulary duplication: lean|rich layered on existing brief|full|verbose creates two overlapping knobs unless the old one is retired in the same change (no-half-implementation risk).

RFC 8785 JCS is partly redundant: serde_json is already sorted-keys (no preserve_order), so the elaborate canonicalization spec buys a small marginal delta — over-engineering risk if the team builds a full JCS layer for what BTreeMap already gives.
eval.rationale: Slice 3 mixes one genuinely good, cheap, defensible change (C6: fold markdown into a MODE param — the dual-channel and a mode arg already exist) with a cluster of premature claims (C4/C5/C8 and density in C9) that are INVALIDATED because their data substrate is Layer A metadata that does not exist (Slices 2/4 greenfield: zero scope_predicate/decay_score/inheritance fields in src/). Building the full envelope now would ship null/placeholder governance fields — the same anti-pattern already visible (snapshot_id:null). Two of the headline claims (canonicalization C1, byte-budget C3) are LOWER-cost and LOWER-marginal-value than the spec implies: serde_json is already sorted-keys, and a token-budget/truncation envelope already ships on the highest-value tool, so Slice 3 partly re-implements existing plumbing in a second vocabulary. The deferred-tool harness observation is mildly relevant here: render:json|json+markdown as MODE (not a new tool) is the right consolidation move, but the rest of the envelope does not reduce cold-start surface — it changes output shape of tools the LLM is mid-flight using, which is the riskier axis. Recommendation: modify_then_build — extract C6 (markdown MODE) + the render_hash/byte-replay determinism harness (C2) as a thin, low-risk first deliverable behind a default-off render param, defer C4/C5/C8/density until Layer A (Slices 2/4) lands, and reconcile the new envelope with the two existing band envelopes rather than adding a third. The empirical replay experiment is the gate: if the tools already replay near-identical after stripping 3 known volatiles, the determinism half of Slice 3 is cheap and worth shipping early; the governance half stays blocked on Layer A regardless."

refute.strongest_counterargument:
The evaluation's residual 38% value rests on exactly two "cheap, defensible" survivors — C6 (markdown-as-MODE) and C2 (render_hash + byte-replay harness). The strongest attack is that BOTH are weaker than scored, which should pull value below 30, not leave it at 38.

(1) C6 is double-counted toward MIL-27's consolidation thesis but does NOT actually reduce the surface the LLM uses in flight. Verified: in THIS client the 67 tools are DEFERRED via ToolSearch (system reminder lists them as not-loaded). markdown_render was never a separate PUBLIC tool here — it is a render mode folded into already-existing dual-channel {content,data} + brief/full/verbose plumbing (format.rs:77 evidence_by_mode, tokens envelope tools_context.rs:682-698). So C6 collapses a tool that the cold-start never paid for anyway. Its consolidation value in the real harness is ~0; its only true value is ergonomic (one param vs one tool), which is marginal. The evaluation tags C6 "validated / cleanest part of the slice" and lets it carry the slice — that is the inflation.

(2) C2's byte-identical-replay harness is a regression GATE for a contract that has no current consumer and no current breakage. I confirmed the substrate: serde_json compiles WITHOUT preserve_order (Cargo.toml has no preserve_order feature) so keys ALREADY sort, and the sort comparators already carry deterministic tiebreakers (tools_context.rs:1510 right.0.cmp + uri tiebreak; :1837 left.uri.cmp). The ONLY real non-determinism is recent_band via `git log --since=24h` (tools_context.rs:647) + cache now_ms + elapsed_ms. So render_hash buys you a hash over output that is ~already deterministic minus 3 known volatiles you must strip anyway — the test proves determinism the system mostly already has, and the headline volatile (recent_band) is exactly the field the hash must EXCLUDE, meaning the hash protects nothing the LLM actually reads for recency. C2 is a solution-in-search-of-a-problem: there is no logged incident of envelope drift breaking a client; building a brittle git-fixture-dependent gate now is speculative.

(3) Blast-radius is UNDER-weighted relative to the realized value. The six targets (inspect/retrieve_context/retrieve_context_v2/why/path/soll_query_context) are the exact tools used every Phase-A onboarding and every code-diagnosis loop. Touching their output shape — even behind a default-off render param — adds a THIRD envelope vocabulary (token-bands REQ-264, tri-modal REQ-91524, now bytes Layer-B) on tools the agent reads mid-flight. The evaluation correctly flags "high blast-radius" but then still scores 38% net-positive; given 4/9 fields are invalidated (greenfield, confirmed: zero density_score/decay_score/scope_predicate/render_hash in src) and the 2 survivors are marginal, the honest expected value of building Slice 3 NOW — before Layer A lands — is net-negative-to-flat, not +38%. The right move is not "modify_then_build" but "defer the whole slice until Layer A (Slices 2/4) lands, and extract ONLY C6 as a trivial ergonomic param decoupled from any envelope/render_hash work." Shipping the determinism harness (C2) now front-loads engineering cost against a contract that does not yet exist and a breakage that has never occurred.

The recommendation (modify_then_build behind default-off param, defer governance fields) is directionally CORRECT and survives — but the value_pct and build-confidence are inflated because the two "keep now" pieces are weaker than scored: C6 has near-zero consolidation value in the deferred-tools harness, and C2 gates a non-existent problem.
refute.notes: Verdict=weakened: the core recommendation (modify_then_build → extract C6 thin, defer C4/C5/C8/density to Layer A, reconcile with existing band envelopes) holds and survives attack — it is the correct shape. But value_pct=38 is inflated and I cut it to 26. Empirically verified main-thread this session: (a) serde_json compiled without preserve_order → keys already sort, so RFC-8785 JCS C1 buys near-nothing; (b) tokens_used/tokens_budget/tokens_overflowed envelope already ships (tools_context.rs:682-698) and snapshot_id:Value::Null placeholder anti-pattern already present (:694), confirming the third-envelope-collision + placeholder risks; (c) char-based truncation marker already in format.rs:94; (d) recent_band via git --since=24h (tools_context.rs:647) is the genuine non-determinism the byte-replay test flakes on; (e) sort comparators already deterministic (uri/id tiebreakers :1510/:1837); (f) zero density_score/decay_score/scope_predicate/render_hash in src/ → C4/C5/C8/density-in-C9 INVALIDATED, greenfield confirmed; (g) GUI-PRO-104 agent-locks is file-edit-level coordination, orthogonal to SOLL-node MVCC, so it does NOT cover this slice's need (that overlap belongs to Slice 4, not Slice 3). Two-pronged downgrade rationale: C6's consolidation value is ~0 in the DEFERRED-tools harness (markdown was never a separately-parsed public tool here, so folding it saves no cold-start tokens) and C2 gates a contract with no current consumer/breakage. Net: build-confidence stays ~30 (matches eval), value drops 38→26 because the 2 survivors are marginal and the 4 invalidated fields make any "build the envelope now" framing net-flat. Experiment proposed in eval (pinned-snapshot double-run byte-diff on ~20 queries stripping 3 volatiles) is the correct gate and worth running before committing to C2; if ≥18/20 already replay-identical the determinism half is cheap but still low-value absent a real consumer. Recommend extracting C6 as a standalone ergonomic param fully decoupled from render_hash/envelope, and deferring the rest behind Layer A.

eval.slice_name: Slice 4 — MVCC expected_revision + conflict_resolution_strategy per node type
expert_claims: OCC is needed: 2 concurrent LLMs editing the same SOLL node will lose updates without expected_revision optimistic-concurrency check.

A mismatch must return a single-call conflict response {current_revision, their_revision, current_hash, their_content_hash, diff_summary<=500B, resolution_strategy} so the loser can act without extra round-trips.
resolution_strategy can be frozen per node-type (Vision/Pillar/Decision=reject, Concept/Requirement=merge_required, Guideline/PromptTemplate/Validation/Stakeholder=last_write_wins).
content_revision auto-increments and content_hash = sha256(description + canonical metadata) gives a deterministic conflict signal.
This is achievable with ZERO migration SQL (DEC-AXO-901623 parent claim: 7 soll.Node columns intact, metadata jsonb only).
expected_revision should be MANDATORY on every soll_manager(update) call.
Acceptance test: 2 concurrent LLMs -> loser receives an actionable conflict.

premise_assessment.claim: OCC is needed: concurrent LLMs lose updates on the same SOLL node without expected_revision.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Empirical: across the entire 179-row soll.RevisionChange history (2 projects, ~12 days, multiple distinct authors codex/claude-opus/claude-sonnet/claude-gpu-expert), same_node_updates_within_60s = 0. The lost-update collision MVCC prevents has NEVER occurred. Write rate ~15/day spread over a large node space => collision probability vanishingly small. The mechanism is architecturally valid but solves a problem this system does not currently have.

premise_assessment.claim: Single-call conflict response lets the loser act without extra round-trips.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Sound design IF a conflict ever happens. Returning current/their revision + hashes + diff_summary + strategy in one envelope is the correct shape for an LLM client (avoids the read-merge-retry storm). The design quality is real; the question is whether the triggering event occurs.

premise_assessment.claim: resolution_strategy frozen per node-type (Vision=reject ... Guideline=last_write_wins).
premise_assessment.reasoning: Plausible but untested against real merge semantics. last_write_wins on Guideline silently discards the loser's edit — for an LLM that just authored a careful revision this is a data-loss footgun, not a resolution. merge_required on Concept assumes a merge primitive that does not exist yet (no field-level 3-way merge in soll_manager). The taxonomy is reasonable on paper but several strategies bottom out in unimplemented or lossy behavior.

premise_assessment.claim: content_revision auto-inc + content_hash sha256(description+canonical metadata) is a deterministic conflict signal.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Technically correct and cheap. content_hash already exists as a pattern elsewhere (IST IndexedFile/Chunk). A monotonic counter + canonical-JSON hash is a standard, correct OCC token. No objection to the mechanism itself.

premise_assessment.claim: ZERO migration SQL — 7 soll.Node columns intact, metadata jsonb only.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Verified soll.Node = exactly 7 columns (id,type,project_code,title,description,status,metadata jsonb), no content_revision/content_hash column. OCC CAS needs an atomic UPDATE...WHERE version=? RETURNING. Storing the counter in metadata jsonb keeps DDL at zero BUT jsonb CAS is non-trivial: `WHERE metadata->>'content_revision' = ?` works yet races unless wrapped in a real transaction. graph_query.rs:531 shows execute_batch (BEGIN;...;COMMIT;) exists, so atomicity is achievable — but the unlink path explicitly notes 'no multi-statement transaction handle' in the convenience API. ZERO-migration is plausible but the atomic-CAS plumbing is more than the claim implies.

premise_assessment.claim: expected_revision MANDATORY on every soll_manager(update).
premise_assessment.reasoning: This is the costly part. Making it mandatory forces a read-before-write round-trip on 100% of updates (the ~99.9% single-agent case) to defend against a collision rate measured at 0. It breaks every existing update call site and every LLM's mental model of soll_manager. If OCC ships at all it should be OPT-IN (pass expected_revision -> enforced; omit -> last-write-wins with audit, which is today's behavior). Mandatory is a net friction tax on the most-used SOLL verb.

premise_assessment.claim: Acceptance test: 2 concurrent LLMs -> loser gets actionable conflict.
premise_assessment.reasoning: The test is well-formed and would pass if built — but it tests a synthetic race that has to be manufactured (no organic occurrence in history). Passing this test proves the mechanism works, not that it is needed.

eval.experiment_needed: Instrument the live + dev soll_manager(update) path with a lightweight collision probe for one full bench/work cycle (or replay): log every (entity_id, author, timestamp) and compute (a) count of same-node updates by DIFFERENT authors within 60s, and (b) count of overwrites where before_json author != prior after_json author with <5min gap. Threshold to cross 70% build_confidence: >=1 real lost-update collision observed, OR a credible near-term scenario where >2 agents are deliberately run concurrently on overlapping SOLL nodes (e.g. the MIL-27 parallel-expert workflow itself writing the same REQ). If the probe stays at 0 collisions (matching the historical 0/179), defer. Cheap variant already run main-thread this session: SQL window query over soll.RevisionChange returned same_node_updates_within_60s = 0.
risks: Mandatory expected_revision breaks every existing single-agent update call and adds a forced read-before-write round-trip to the most-used SOLL verb, taxing 99.9% of traffic to defend a 0-observed-collision case.

Overlap/duplication with GUI-PRO-104 (planned 'Multi-LLM concurrent edit coordination protocol') and file-level GUI-PRO-104 agent-locks: two coordination mechanisms for the same multi-LLM problem risks contradictory or redundant semantics. Reconcile ownership before building.

last_write_wins for Guideline/PromptTemplate silently discards the loser's authored revision — data-loss footgun dressed as a resolution strategy. merge_required for Concept/Requirement assumes a field-level merge primitive that does not exist in soll_manager today.

ZERO-migration claim is fragile: atomic CAS on a counter living in metadata jsonb requires real transactional UPDATE...WHERE...RETURNING; the convenience API lacks a transaction handle (per the unlink-path comment), so correctness needs the execute_batch BEGIN;...;COMMIT; path wired in — more plumbing than 'metadata only' suggests.

Building OCC now front-loads complexity onto a contract (v4) that is still greenfield; an over-engineered concurrency layer for a single-writer-in-practice system is premature optimization that raises the cognitive cost of every future soll_manager change.

Deferred-tools harness observation does NOT mitigate here: this slice touches the runtime mutation path, not cold-start/consolidation, so the friction lands at call time on the hottest write verb regardless of how tools are loaded.
eval.rationale: Mechanism is technically correct and the conflict-response shape is well-designed, but the empirical foundation is absent: 0 same-node-within-60s updates across 179 historical revisions means the lost-update collision MVCC prevents has never occurred under real multi-agent usage. content_hash/content_revision/expected_revision are confirmed greenfield (soll.Node = 7 columns, no version field). An append-only audit journal (soll.Revision/RevisionChange) already exists but is not OCC. The high blast radius comes almost entirely from the MANDATORY framing, which would force a read-before-write on ~99.9% of single-agent updates and break every call site. Recommendation: do not build the mandatory full taxonomy now. If built, build OPT-IN (pass expected_revision -> enforced CAS via execute_batch transaction; omit -> current last-write-wins + audit), drop the per-type frozen strategy table until a merge primitive exists, and first reconcile scope with GUI-PRO-104. The decisive gate is the collision probe: only a real observed collision (or a deliberately concurrent MIL-27 workflow on overlapping nodes) justifies crossing 70% confidence. value_pct 22 and build_confidence 28 reflect a sound mechanism aimed at a problem the system does not yet demonstrably have."

refute.strongest_counterargument:
The eval set a falsifiable gate ("cross 70% confidence only on >=1 real observed lost-update collision OR a credible near-term concurrent-write scenario") and then failed to apply the gate's verdict to its own headline numbers. I re-ran and deepened the probe against the LIVE knowledge base and the gate is not merely unmet — it is structurally unmeetable under current usage, which means value_pct 22 is still INFLATED and "modify_then_build" is too generous; the honest call is "defer / do_not_build_now."

EVIDENCE (live, this session):
1. Collisions: same-node-within-60s = 0; same-node-DIFFERENT-author-within-5min = 0 (corrected query JOINing soll.revisionchange→soll.revision for authorship, since revisionchange has NO author column — the original eval's "author" reasoning relied on a column that does not exist on that table, yet the conclusion still holds). The minimum gap between any two changes to the SAME node-content entity is ~82 MINUTES (REQ-AXO-057, 4,902,142 ms); the only sub-60s same-node events are bulk edge unlink/relink operations by a single author, not content races.
2. The ONLY concurrent-edit incident in the entire SOLL corpus — REQ-AXO-328, which spawned GUI-PRO-104 — is a SOURCE-FILE collision (`retrieval_model.rs`, Track B silently dropped Track A's `fts_rank` field), NOT a soll_manager(update) collision. There is zero observed SOLL-node lost-update in history. So the "real problem" the experts invoke has only ever materialized at the file layer, where GUI-PRO-104 already owns it.
3. The eval's escape hatch — "credible near-term scenario: the MIL-27 parallel-expert workflow writes the same REQ" — is FALSE by construction. This very workflow shards one REQ per agent (this agent owns REQ-AXO-901787 alone); the parallel experts write DIFFERENT node IDs. The orchestration that would supposedly trigger the race is itself single-writer-per-node. There is no realistic path to the triggering event.

WHY THIS LOWERS VALUE rather than raising it: a devil's advocate could note GUI-PRO-104 is file-level (so the eval's "overlap/duplication" RISK is overstated — these are not redundant mechanisms, they cover different layers). True. But removing that risk does not add value to Slice 4; it removes the only place the problem has EVER appeared and shows it is being solved elsewhere by a cheaper, already-specified mechanism. The net effect on value is downward, not upward: the one empirical anchor for "lost updates happen" points at a layer Slice 4 does not even touch.

BLAST RADIUS is if anything UNDER-stated, and not only via the MANDATORY framing the eval already flagged. Even OPT-IN OCC (the eval's recommended fallback) permanently couples the hottest write verb (soll_manager update) to a content_revision/content_hash counter that must be (a) atomically CAS'd inside an execute_batch BEGIN;...;COMMIT (the convenience API has no transaction handle), (b) kept in sync with the existing append-only soll.revision journal which is a SEPARATE write path — creating two sources of "what revision is this node at" that can silently diverge, and (c) embedded into the v4 content_hash = sha256(description + canonical metadata), which transitively drags in the Layer-A canonicalization/JCS work from DEC-AXO-901623 before OCC can even be correct. That is a multi-slice coupling hidden behind "ZERO migration SQL." The deferred-tools harness observation gives no relief here (correctly noted) — this is runtime mutation cost, paid on every guarded write forever.

CONCLUSION: value_pct should drop from 22 toward ~12 and confidence toward ~20. Recommendation should move from "modify_then_build" to "defer": keep the design on the shelf (the conflict-response envelope shape IS good and cheap to spec), but do NOT build even opt-in OCC until (i) a real SOLL-node lost-update is observed, OR (ii) a deliberately concurrent same-node SOLL workflow is actually deployed. Until then, the existing append-only soll.revision audit journal already provides post-hoc detectability (you can SEE an overwrite after the fact), which is the only capability this single-writer system demonstrably needs.
refute.notes:
Verdict = weakened (not refuted): the eval's direction is RIGHT (don't build the mandatory full taxonomy now; mechanism is technically sound) and its empirical spine survives independent re-verification — but its magnitudes are too generous and one risk is mis-scoped. Corrections the eval should absorb:

SURVIVES: (a) 0 collisions confirmed independently — even strengthened (min same-node-content gap = 82 min, not just <60s window empty). (b) Mandatory-expected_revision is a real friction tax — agreed. (c) content_hash/content_revision/expected_revision are greenfield — confirmed soll.node = exactly 7 columns [id,type,project_code,title,description,status,metadata jsonb]; live brain reports 1736 nodes already carrying metadata jsonb. (d) Single-call conflict envelope is well-shaped — agreed, keep the spec.

WHERE THE EVAL IS WRONG / SOFT:
1. value_pct 22 and confidence 28 are inflated. The eval's OWN gate (>=1 real collision OR credible concurrent scenario) is structurally unmeetable: 0 SOLL-node collisions EVER, and the MIL-27 parallel-expert workflow shards one-REQ-per-agent so it cannot self-trigger the race. A gate that cannot be crossed by current architecture argues for lower value, not 22%. Adjusted to value 13 / confidence 20.
2. The GUI-PRO-104 "overlap/duplication" risk is OVERSTATED. GUI-PRO-104 claims absolute repo-relative FILE paths and was born from a source-file incident (REQ-AXO-328: retrieval_model.rs, dropped fts_rank field). It is file-layer coordination; it does NOT cover soll_manager(update) MVCC. So they are not redundant — but this REMOVES the only empirical evidence that "lost updates happen at all," because that evidence lives at a layer Slice 4 doesn't touch. Net: value down, not the reconciliation-needed risk the eval framed.
3. Recommendation should move modify_then_build → defer. "Modify then build" still greenlights building opt-in OCC, which permanently couples the hottest write verb to a counter that must be atomically CAS'd (no txn handle in convenience API per the unlink-path comment), kept in sync with the SEPARATE append-only soll.revision journal (two-sources-of-truth divergence risk), and dragged into the v4 JCS content_hash — multi-slice coupling masked by "ZERO migration SQL." Defer; the existing append-only journal already gives post-hoc overwrite detection, the only capability a single-writer system demonstrably needs.

HONEST CAVEAT (kept me from refuted): the audit journal only captures COMMITTED revisions; a write path that bypasses soll.revision would be invisible to the collision probe. I could not exhaustively prove every soll_manager(update) writes a revision row. If a silent (unjournaled) write path exists, the 0-collision evidence weakens and value recovers toward the eval's 22. That residual uncertainty is why this is weakened, not refuted.

expert_claims: Routing accuracy of LLMs degrades non-linearly above 25-30 tools (Anthropic perspective); effective working set ≤20 tools/session (OpenAI perspective) — therefore collapsing 67→≤15 surfaced tools materially improves tool-selection accuracy.
Cold-start tax of ~200-800 tokens × 67 tools = 15-50K tokens parsed before the first tool call; consolidation reclaims this budget.
Demoting 55+ tools to action= sub-params on 12 core orthogonal verbs preserves all capability (tools demoted, not deleted) while shrinking the choice surface.
Graceful deprecation discipline (feature flag + parallel old+new surface ≥1 bench cycle + deprecation warnings ≥1 sprint + help(tool=demoted) redirect) guarantees zero LLM session broken mid-flight.
mcp_surface_diagnostics reporting ≤15 surfaced tools is a valid, sufficient acceptance oracle for the consolidation.
The consolidation can be executed safely as one slice over the existing dispatcher (renames + action-param discrimination on the central match).

premise_assessment.claim: Routing accuracy degrades non-linearly above 25-30 tools; ≤15 surfaced improves accuracy.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Directionally plausible and well-supported in the broader literature, BUT not measured for THIS surface in THIS harness. Critical confound: in Claude Code the 67 tools are DEFERRED (ToolSearch), so the LLM never sees 67 schemas at once — it queries by intent and gets ≤5 back. The effective working set is ALREADY small here. The accuracy claim holds strongly only for clients that eagerly load all schemas (many do). So the benefit is real but client-conditional, not universal. No in-tree benchmark (routing accuracy vs surface size) exists to validate the magnitude.

premise_assessment.claim: Cold-start tax 15-50K tokens × 67 reclaimed by consolidation.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Directly contradicted by the harness observation I confirmed this session: all 67 axon tools arrived as DEFERRED via ToolSearch, not eagerly parsed at cold-start. In Claude Code the cold-start pays the ToolSearch index cost, not 67 full schemas. The 15-50K figure assumes an eager-loading client. For the operator's actual primary client the reclaimed budget is near-zero. The premise is true for SOME MCP clients (eager loaders) but false for the one in active use — so as stated (a flat 15-50K win) it is invalidated.

premise_assessment.claim: Demote-not-delete via action= params on 12 verbs preserves capability.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Mechanically sound and already precedented in-tree. soll_manager (catalog.rs:86) already discriminates action={create,update,link,unlink} over one handler. execute_tool_direct (mcp.rs:1167) is a flat match name => self.axon_X — adding alias arms + action-discriminated entries that route to the same handlers is trivial and lossless. is_public_tool + tools_catalog(include_internal) already separate surfaced vs callable. Capability preservation is real.

premise_assessment.claim: Graceful deprecation guarantees zero LLM session broken mid-flight.
premise_assessment.reasoning: Partly contradicted by the codebase's own constraints. (a) The slice ignores the project's OWN gate: tests/runtime_surface.rs asserts ~30+ specific tool NAMES are present in the public surface (lines 26-55) — demoting them breaks the test suite before any LLM is involved; the slice must rewrite those assertions, which is in-scope but unstated. (b) 'Zero session broken' for a still-connected LLM that already cached the old 67-tool binding is NOT guaranteed by server-side aliasing alone — if a tool is removed from the advertised surface, a mid-flight client may have a tool the server now rejects (or vice-versa). The existing client_binding_notes in tools_framework_surface.rs already documents this exact stale-binding hazard. Server keeps old names DISPATCHABLE (aliases) so calls still succeed even if no longer advertised — that is the real safety mechanism, and it is achievable, but the guarantee depends on dispatch-alias retention, not on the deprecation-warning theater.

premise_assessment.claim: mcp_surface_diagnostics ≤15 is a sufficient acceptance oracle.
premise_assessment.reasoning: It is a NECESSARY oracle and already exists (computes public_tool_count from tools_catalog(false)). But it is NOT sufficient: it proves the count, not that routing accuracy improved or that no capability regressed. A green ≤15 with degraded task success would be a false pass. The real acceptance signal — routing accuracy / task-completion delta — is unmeasured and has no harness.

premise_assessment.claim: Executable safely as one slice over the existing dispatcher.
premise_assessment.reasoning: The dispatch architecture genuinely supports it: single centralized match (mcp.rs:1167), existing prefix-stripping aliasing (dispatch.rs:12-15), existing public/internal catalog split, existing action-param precedent. The mechanical edit is low-difficulty. What makes it VERY HIGH blast-radius is not the code change but that it mutates the live contract every connected LLM (Claude/Codex/Gemini) depends on — including this evaluator mid-task.

eval.experiment_needed: Routing-accuracy A/B on the real surface, BEFORE any consolidation code. Construct ~40 representative task prompts with a known-correct tool. Run them against (A) the current 67-tool surface and (B) a simulated ≤15-verb surface (just a stub catalog + help text — no dispatcher changes), in BOTH an eager-loading MCP client and Claude Code's deferred/ToolSearch mode. Measure first-call tool-selection accuracy and tokens-to-first-call in each cell. Build-now threshold passes (≥70%) only if the ≤15 surface shows a material accuracy gain (>10 pts) OR token saving (>10K) in the operator's ACTUAL client (Claude Code, deferred). If the gain only appears in eager clients — which the harness observation predicts — defer or rescope to a client-conditional surface profile rather than a hard 67→15 migration.
risks: VERY HIGH blast-radius on the live MCP contract: this is the surface every connected LLM (Claude/Codex/Gemini) routes through, including the evaluating agent mid-flight. A bad cutover degrades all sessions simultaneously, not one feature.

The headline justification (15-50K cold-start tax) is INVALIDATED for the operator's primary client (Claude Code defers tools). Building for a benefit that does not materialize in the client actually used is the dominant value risk.
Self-inflicted test breakage: tests/runtime_surface.rs hard-asserts ~30+ tool names present; consolidation breaks the suite first. Unstated in the slice scope.

Stale client-binding hazard (already documented in tools_framework_surface.rs client_binding_notes): a mid-flight LLM cached the 67-tool advertisement; demoting tools mid-session can produce 'tool not found' or schema-mismatch unless old names stay dispatchable as aliases indefinitely — which dilutes the 'cleaner surface' goal.
Oracle gap: ≤15 surfaced is a count, not a quality signal. Risk of a green acceptance test masking a routing-accuracy or capability regression that only shows up in real task throughput.

Premature-elegance risk: a 67→15 collapse is architecturally satisfying but, given the deferred-tools harness, may deliver near-zero real LLM benefit while consuming a high-risk migration budget and permanently widening the alias-maintenance surface.

Coupling to MIL-27 slices not yet built (no env_status/self-introspection tool, no consolidation flag): executing the surface cut before the introspection tools that would let an LLM self-diagnose a broken binding leaves no in-band recovery path if the cutover misbehaves.
eval.rationale: The slice is mechanically easy and architecturally clean (centralized dispatcher, existing action-param precedent in soll_manager, existing public/internal catalog split, existing prefix-aliasing, existing mcp_surface_diagnostics oracle). What it is NOT is empirically justified for the client actually in use. The two load-bearing expert premises split: 'demote-not-delete preserves capability' is validated, but the economic headline 'cold-start tax 15-50K × 67' is invalidated by the confirmed deferred-tools behavior of Claude Code — the 67 tools arrived via ToolSearch this very session, not eagerly. The routing-accuracy premise is real in the literature but unmeasured for this surface and likely muted in the deferred client. Combined with VERY HIGH blast-radius on the live contract every LLM depends on (including this agent mid-task) and an acceptance oracle (≤15 count) that proves the count but not the benefit, build-now is not warranted. The correct move is a cheap, code-free routing-accuracy + tokens-to-first-call A/B across eager vs deferred clients BEFORE touching the dispatcher; that experiment is what flips the 70% gate. If the gain shows up only in eager clients, rescope to a client-conditional surface profile rather than a hard, irreversible 67→15 migration. value_pct 32 and build_confidence 28 reflect real-but-client-conditional value against a high-risk, justification-weakened build."]

refute.strongest_counterargument:
The eval is too generous on BOTH the value (32) and the recommendation framing ("build_after_experiment"), because the harness has ALREADY solved the exact problem the consolidation targets — making the experiment a near-foregone failure for the operator's real client and the build value near-zero there.

CORE ATTACK — the need is already covered by ToolSearch, not by consolidation. Confirmed empirically THIS session: ~80 axon tools arrived DEFERRED via ToolSearch, and the documented ToolSearch contract returns ≤5 schemas per intent query. So the LLM's *effective working routing surface is already ≤5* — well below the consolidation's own ≤15 target. The premise the whole slice rests on (Anthropic: accuracy degrades >25-30 tools; OpenAI: ≤20/session) is satisfied for free in this client by deferral. Consolidating 67→15 cannot improve first-call routing accuracy below a surface that is already ≤5. The eval marks the routing premise "uncertain/client-conditional" but stops short of the harder conclusion: for the operator's actual client the routing benefit is not merely muted, it is structurally pre-empted. That collapses the value, not just qualifies it.

SECOND ATTACK — the one VALIDATED premise ("demote-not-delete preserves capability") actively defeats the goal and the eval credits it as a plus. Verified in-tree: is_public_tool (catalog.rs:5) currently returns true for EVERYTHING except one tool — the surface is "advertise all," so consolidation is not a config flip but hand-authoring a 12-verb catalog + retrofitting 50+ handlers into action= discrimination + keeping all 55 demoted names dispatchable as aliases indefinitely (the only real anti-stale-binding safety, per client_binding_notes in tools_framework_surface.rs:99-116). Result: the dispatch table, the catalog ToolSearch indexes, and the maintenance surface all stay the same size or grow (two names per tool, permanent alias arms). The headline "cleaner surface" is cosmetic — only the advertised count drops; real complexity rises. So even the validated premise argues AGAINST the build's stated benefit.

THIRD ATTACK — blast-radius is understated even at very_high, and the eval missed the magnitude. runtime_surface.rs has 206 `contains` assertions (not "~30"), all hard-coding tool names that consolidation breaks before any LLM is touched. Against that self-inflicted regression cost, the live contract risk for 3 LLMs (incl. this evaluator mid-flight), and a benefit the harness already delivers for free, the risk/value ratio is far worse than 32 implies.

WHERE THE EVAL IS RIGHT (kept): "executable as one slice" mechanically true; mcp_surface_diagnostics oracle exists but proves only the count. The eval's defer-don't-build instinct is correct — but it dresses the experiment as a possible path to "build," when the deferred-client outcome is effectively pre-determined to fail the gate. The experiment is worth running once (cheap, code-free) only to formally document that the harness already wins — not as a genuine fork toward building.

NET: value should drop from 32 (the dominant real-client benefit is pre-empted by ToolSearch, and the one validated premise inflates rather than reduces complexity); confidence drops modestly (greenfield + no in-tree routing benchmark keeps residual uncertainty, but the deferred-tools fact is now empirically nailed twice). Recommendation survives as "defer / experiment-then-almost-certainly-don't-build," but the eval's implicit door to "build_after_experiment flips green for this client" is refuted.
refute.notes: Verified in-tree this session: (1) is_public_tool (catalog.rs:5) returns true for all but resume_vectorization → surface is "advertise all," so 67→15 requires authoring a 12-verb catalog + retrofitting 50+ handlers into action= discrimination, not a flag flip. (2) runtime_surface.rs has 206 `contains` assertions hard-coding tool names — self-inflicted test break is ~7x larger than the eval's "~30+." (3) client_binding_notes (tools_framework_surface.rs:99-116) confirms the stale-binding hazard; indefinite dispatch-aliasing is the only real safety, which negates the maintenance-surface "win." (4) Empirically reconfirmed: ~80 axon tools arrived DEFERRED via ToolSearch this session, returning ≤5 per intent — so the effective routing surface is already ≤5, below the ≤15 target, pre-empting the dominant benefit in the operator's actual client. Recommendation to defer survives; value lowered 32→18 because the real-client benefit is structurally pre-empted and the lone validated premise (demote-not-delete) raises rather than lowers complexity; confidence 28→24 (greenfield + no in-tree routing benchmark, but deferred-tools fact now empirically nailed). The experiment is worth one cheap code-free run only to document that the harness already wins, not as a genuine fork to "build."

eval.slice_name: Slice 6 — Pilote v4 (3 PIL + 5 GUI + 2 REQ) + mesure cold-start ±20%
expert_claims: CLAIM A: Encoder 3 Pillars + 5 Guidelines + 2 REQ en v4 fait passer le cold-start LLM de 15-20K tokens à 6-8K tokens (±20%) — un facteur ~2.5x de compression.

CLAIM B: Layer B (envelope JSON canonique short≤500 + rules[] + adjacent_context borné) réduit les round-trips vs les anciens 'dump tools' (soll_query_context / retrieve_context renvoyant des bodies prose entiers).
CLAIM C: Le replay byte-identical sur fixed inputs (render_hash excluant volatiles, RFC 8785 JCS sorted-keys) est vérifiable et stable — preuve de déterminisme du rendu.

CLAIM D: Le pilote restreint est une GATE conditionnelle valide : si la mesure tombe hors bande ±20%, on itère Layer A/B AVANT la migration de masse (Slice 7), évitant de migrer en masse sur une hypothèse fausse.
CLAIM E (implicite, hérité de DEC-901624): le cold-start tax provient pour l'essentiel du volume de tokens parsés au boot, donc le compresser a une valeur LLM directe.

premise_assessment.claim: CLAIM A — cold-start 15-20K → 6-8K tokens (±20%) sur le corpus pilote
premise_assessment.reasoning: Mesuré main-thread : le corpus pilote réel (PIL-AXO-001=2477c, PIL-AXO-003=2264c, PIL-AXO-9003=8152c, 5 GUI=7758c, 2 REQ≈1762c) = ~22 400 chars ≈ 5 600 tokens BRUTS. Donc le baseline '15-20K tokens' de la cible expert ne correspond PAS aux bodies SOLL du pilote — il conflate le tool-schema parsing tax (15-50K, prémisse de DEC-901624) avec les body reads SOLL. Sur le vrai corpus pilote, le baseline est ~5-6K tokens, pas 15-20K. La cible '6-8K après' est donc déjà ~= le baseline brut : il n'y a pas un facteur 2.5x à gagner sur CE corpus. CEPENDANT, un node comme PIL-AXO-9003 (8152 chars, prose dense) compressé en short≤500+rules[] descendrait vers ~1-1.5K chars : compression réelle locale 3-5x sur les nodes bloated. Le chiffre agrégé '15-20K→6-8K' est mal calibré, mais la direction (compression substantielle des nodes prose-lourds) est plausible. C'est exactement ce que la slice doit MESURER — d'où uncertain, pas invalidated.

premise_assessment.claim: CLAIM B — Layer B réduit les round-trips vs dump tools
premise_assessment.reasoning: Plausible mais non prouvé sans build. retrieve_context_layered existe déjà (mcp/tools_context.rs:579, 3 bands en 1 call) — donc une partie de la réduction de round-trips est DÉJÀ livrée par l'infra existante, pas par v4. La valeur incrémentale de Layer B sur les round-trips dépend de adjacent_context (parents≤5/children≤10) qui n'existe pas encore. Mesurable seulement après Slice 3 (901786, status=current, non livré).

premise_assessment.claim: CLAIM C — replay byte-identical via render_hash / JCS
premise_assessment.reasoning: Techniquement solide et standard : RFC 8785 JCS + sha256 excluant les champs volatiles est un pattern éprouvé (déterminisme de sérialisation). C'est la partie la moins risquée. Réserve : ça teste le déterminisme du RENDU, pas la valeur LLM — un hash stable ne prouve aucun gain de tokens ni de routing. Vérité d'ingénierie, pas vérité produit.

premise_assessment.claim: CLAIM D — pilote = GATE conditionnelle avant migration de masse
premise_assessment.reasoning: C'est la prémisse méthodologiquement la plus forte de toute la milestone. Mesurer sur 10 nodes réversibles avant de migrer N centaines de nodes est exactement la discipline empirique correcte (et conforme au feedback opérateur 'expérience main-thread avant build de masse'). Le blast-radius low est crédible : 10 nodes, metadata jsonb additif, zéro migration SQL, rollback via soll_rollback_revision. Cette slice EST l'expérience qui dé-risque Slice 7.

premise_assessment.claim: CLAIM E — cold-start tax dominé par tokens parsés au boot
premise_assessment.reasoning: Pour la moitié 'tool consolidation' c'est invalidé par l'observation harness : dans Claude Code (client réel), les 67 tools MCP sont DEFERRED via ToolSearch — confirmé dans CETTE session (les schémas n'apparaissent qu'à la demande). Le cold-start ne paie donc PAS '200-800 tokens × 67'. MAIS cette slice-6 ne touche PAS les tool-schemas — elle mesure les BODIES SOLL, qui eux SONT lus eagerly au boot (GUI-PRO-102 Phase A lit les bodies). Donc E est invalidé pour la justification 'tool tax' mais le coût body-read que CETTE slice attaque est réel et indépendant des deferred-tools. La conflation des deux dans le baseline 15-20K est précisément ce qui rend CLAIM A mal calibré.

eval.experiment_needed: Expérience main-thread en 2 temps, faisable AUJOURD'HUI sans build (dé-risque le baseline avant tout code) : (1) BASELINE — tokeniser le corpus pilote réel (les 10 bodies déjà extraits ce thread : 22 400 chars) avec le tokenizer cible, pour figer le 'avant' exact en tokens (pas l'estimation chars/4). Résultat attendu ~5-6K tokens, ce qui INVALIDE déjà le baseline expert 15-20K et force à recalibrer la cible. (2) DRY-RUN ENCODE — réécrire À LA MAIN 2 nodes extrêmes (PIL-AXO-9003 8152 chars = pire cas prose, et GUI-PRO-013 192 chars = déjà compact) au format Layer A (short≤500 + rules[] + rationale + anchors[]) SANS jsonschema ni code, juste le JSON cible, et tokeniser l'envelope rendu. Le seuil 70% est franchi SI : le ratio de compression médian sur ces 2 nodes ≥ 2.0x ET le contenu reste sémantiquement suffisant pour qu'un LLM agisse (test : un LLM frais peut-il dériver l'intent du node depuis le short+rules seuls sans le body prose ?). Si compression médiane <1.5x OU perte d'intent → la slice complète (et donc Slice 7) doit être re-spécifiée, pas buildée.
risks: Baseline mal calibré : la cible '15-20K→6-8K' conflate tool-schema tax (15-50K, déjà neutralisé par deferred-tools dans le client réel) et body-read tax (~5-6K réels sur le pilote). Risque que la slice 'passe' ou 'échoue' contre un baseline fictif et que la GATE de Slice 7 s'appuie sur un chiffre faux. À corriger AVANT toute mesure : recalibrer le baseline sur les vrais bodies.

Dépendance bloquante non livrée : Slice 6 a besoin de Slice 3 (901786, Layer B envelope/render_hash/scope_predicate) et Slice 4 (901787, MVCC) — toutes deux status=current, NON livrées. Un 'pilote v4' complet (avec round-trips Layer B et replay render_hash) ne peut PAS s'exécuter avant elles. Risque de séquencement : la slice est annoncée 'low blast / réversible' mais son coût réel inclut implicitement le build de Slices 2/3/4.

Encodage manuel non-représentatif : encoder 10 nodes À LA MAIN par un humain/LLM expert produit une compression optimiste qui ne se reproduira PAS quand soll_manager(action=create) l'imposera mécaniquement à des centaines de nodes hétérogènes. Risque de sur-extrapoler 10 best-cases vers la masse.

Perte d'intent silencieuse : compresser PIL-AXO-9003 (8152→~1.2K chars) peut larguer du contexte que le body portait réellement (la slice GUI-AXO-1024 prévoit un fallback 'description text si concept vraiment non-standard' — mais le juge de 'vraiment non-standard' est subjectif). Risque que la compression mesure des tokens mais dégrade la capacité d'action LLM, non mesurée par le seul ratio bytes.

Mesure de round-trips ambiguë : retrieve_context_layered (déjà livré, 3 bands/1 call) capture déjà une partie du gain round-trip ; isoler la contribution PROPRE de Layer B exige un A/B contrôlé, sinon le crédit est attribué à tort à v4.
eval.rationale: Slice 6 est méthodologiquement la slice la PLUS saine de MIL-27 : c'est explicitement une GATE empirique réversible (blast-radius low confirmé — 10 nodes, metadata jsonb additif, zéro migration SQL, rollback soll_rollback_revision) dont la raison d'être est de mesurer avant de migrer en masse (Slice 7). Sur la valeur réelle : la compression des nodes prose-lourds est crédible et utile (PIL-AXO-9003 = 8152 chars de prose dense est un cas d'école), d'où value_pct=72. MAIS la cible expert chiffrée '15-20K→6-8K tokens' est mal calibrée — vérifié main-thread, le vrai corpus pilote ≈ 22 400 chars ≈ 5-6K tokens, parce que le baseline expert conflate le tool-schema tax (neutralisé par les deferred-tools dans Claude Code, observation harness confirmée ce thread) avec le body-read tax. Construire le pilote MAINTENANT contre un baseline faux risquerait de fausser la GATE de Slice 7. Le bon move : exécuter d'abord l'expérience main-thread (tokeniser le baseline réel + dry-run encoder 2 nodes extrêmes à la main, ZÉRO code requis) pour recalibrer la cible et confirmer un ratio de compression ≥2x sans perte d'intent — puis builder. build_confidence=68 (pas plus haut car bloqué par Slices 3/4 non livrées et baseline à corriger ; pas plus bas car la slice elle-même est la bonne discipline empirique). recommendation = build_after_experiment.

refute.strongest_counterargument:
Le value_pct=72 est gonflé parce que l'expérience que la slice propose, si elle est exécutée fidèlement, REFUTE très probablement la raison d'être de la slice plutôt que de la confirmer — et plusieurs faits vérifiés main-thread cette session le montrent :

(1) LA GATE EST LOGIQUEMENT INCAPABLE DE PRODUIRE UN SIGNAL. Corpus pilote réel = PIL-AXO-001(2477c)+PIL-AXO-003(2264c)+PIL-AXO-9003(8152c)+PIL-AXO-9002(925c)+GUI-PRO-013(192c)+5 GUI+2 REQ ≈ 22 400 chars ≈ 5 600 tokens (chars/4, le tokenizer que le code utilise réellement, tools_context.rs:4142 `chars().count()/4+1`). La cible expert « après » = 6-8K tokens. Donc la cible de SUCCÈS (6-8K) est AU-DESSUS du baseline réel (~5,6K). Une GATE dont la bande de succès dépasse son propre baseline ne peut pas « échouer informativement » : le facteur 2.5x est une fiction issue de la conflation tool-schema-tax (15-50K) et body-read-tax (~5,6K). L'évaluation reconnaît la mauvaise calibration mais s'arrête avant la conclusion tueuse : le gain AGRÉGÉ réel sur le pilote = compresser ~5,6K vers peut-être ~3,5K = ~2K tokens/session, un bruit de fond contre un contexte 200K-1M. Une expérience disciplinée pour mesurer un gain de 2K tokens ne justifie pas de migrer des centaines de nodes en Slice 7.

(2) BLAST-RADIUS SOUS-ESTIMÉ + INVERSION cheap/gate-valid. L'évaluation dit « bloqué par Slices 3/4 » ; le corps SOLL de 901790 dit « bloqué par Slice 5 (901789 tool consolidation) ». Aucun des deux n'est complet : les cibles de mesure PROPRES de la slice — (b) round-trips Layer B vs dump, (c) replay byte-identical render_hash — exigent Slice 3 (901786, envelope+render_hash, status=current NON livré), et l'encodage v4 via soll_manager(action=create, metadata.schema_version='v4') exige Slice 2 (901785, crate jsonschema ABSENTE de Cargo.toml, vérifié). Donc un pilote v4 FIDÈLE est transitivement bloqué par 2+3+5. Le « low blast / réversible » n'est vrai que pour la version dégradée (encoder 10 nodes à la main, estimer les tokens à l'œil) — exactement la version qui NE PEUT PAS mesurer (b) ni (c), donc ne peut pas gater Slice 7 sur les critères que la slice elle-même nomme. L'évaluation crédite le faible coût de la version cheap ET la valeur-gate de la version full : ce sont deux objets différents.

(3) LE ROUND-TRIP (CLAIM B) EST DÉJÀ LIVRÉ. retrieve_context_layered (tools_context.rs:579) fournit déjà 3-band single-call, budgets par bande (intent=2000/code=6000/recent=1500), troncature, et `tokens_pre_truncation` vs post — soit exactement la mesure « avant/après tokens » que Slice 6 vend comme livrable. La valeur incrémentale de v4 sur les round-trips est quasi-nulle tant que adjacent_context (parents≤5/children≤10) n'existe pas = Slice 3.

Bilan : seuls CLAIM D (discipline gate = méthode saine) et CLAIM C (render déterministe = vérité d'ingénierie, « pas vérité produit » de l'aveu même de l'éval) survivent. Une slice dont le chiffre quantifié est fictif, dont la moitié round-trip est pré-livrée, et dont la seule valeur robuste est « la méthode est disciplinée » ne mérite pas 72. Méthode saine ≠ slice à forte valeur.
refute.notes:
La recommandation build_after_experiment SURVIT directionnellement — exécuter d'abord l'expérience cheap (recalibrer le baseline réel + dry-run encoder 2 nodes extrêmes, ZÉRO code) reste le bon move et c'est confirmé par les faits. Ce qui est WEAKENED : (a) le value_pct doit chuter de 72 à ~48 ; (b) le CADRAGE de l'expérience doit s'inverser — elle n'est pas « dé-risquer pour ENABLER la migration de masse », elle est « probe peu coûteux qui va très probablement REFUTER la prémisse de Slice 7 sur le corpus pilote » (gain agrégé ~2K tokens, headline fictif, round-trip pré-livré).

FAITS VÉRIFIÉS MAIN-THREAD CETTE SESSION : (1) Greenfield confirmé — pas de jsonschema dans Cargo.toml, aucun scope_predicate/render_hash/expected_revision/decay_score dans src/. (2) retrieve_context_layered EXISTE et livre déjà le 3-band budget + pre/post token metering via chars/4 (tools_context.rs:579-718, estimate_tokens:4141). (3) GUI-PRO-102 Phase A step 6 lit CHAQUE Pillar body en entier au cold-start → la body-read tax du pilote (PIL-AXO-9003=8152c) est RÉELLE et indépendante des deferred-tools — donc l'observation harness #2 ne tue PAS cette slice, contrairement à la moitié tool-consolidation ; sur ce point précis l'évaluation a raison. (4) Chaîne de deps réelle : Slice1(901788) DELIVERED ; Slices 2/3/4/5 toutes status=current NON livrées ; un pilote FIDÈLE (b)+(c) exige 2+3+5.

Mais cette valeur est « éviter une migration inutile », pas « 2.5x compression », d'où value 48 et pas 72.

premise_assessment.reasoning: Ordre défendable et empiriquement cohérent : Vision (28) + Pillars (94) sont les nodes les plus cités par les tools panoramiques et l'init — leur ROI de migration est le plus élevé par node. C'est exactement ce que slice 6 doit produire — d'où le gate."
risks: Construire maintenant viole le gate de mesure du propre milestone : slice 6 existe pour valider la prédiction expert ±20% AVANT de toucher 589 nodes. Mass-migrer en premier = jeter la valeur de l'expérience contrôlée.

Dépendance dure non satisfaite : le mécanisme central (soll_validate flag debt + decay_score auto-resurface) repose sur Layer A lifecycle (slices 2/3) — 0/1736 nodes ont schema_version/content_hash/decay_score aujourd'hui. La slice est non-constructible tant que 2/3/6 ne sont pas livrées.

Coût humain réel non chiffré : le grain '1 node/session' implique des centaines de sessions de review. Sans mesure du coût/node (slice 6), le budget de la slice 7 est inconnu.

Faux sentiment de complétude : flagger les non-migrés en 'dette decay' avant que la valeur soit prouvée transforme une base saine (prose qui marche aujourd'hui) en dette artificielle auto-resurfacée — pollution du signal soll_validate.
eval.rationale: Slice 7 est la slice TERMINALE de MIL-27 et doit le rester dans l'ordre d'exécution. Trois faits empiriques vérifiés main-thread la disqualifient pour un build immédiat : (1) toute la chaîne amont (slices 2,3,4,5,6) est 'current'/unbuilt — son mécanisme central (decay_score auto-resurface via soll_validate) n'a aucun support : 0/1736 nodes portent un champ v4 ; (2) slice 6 est un gate de mesure DÉLIBÉRÉ : migrer 589 nodes avant d'avoir mesuré la prédiction expert ±20% sur 10 nodes contredit la stratégie de validation du milestone lui-même ; (3) metadata jsonb est déjà peuplée à 97.9% avec 33 clés legacy, donc la migration est une FUSION risquée (collision/idempotence sur une base de connaissance NEVER-delete), pas un remplissage vierge comme le suppose l'expert. L'observation harness deferred-tools est ici quasi neutre : slice 7 ne touche pas la surface MCP ni le cold-start, donc ne bénéficie d'aucune des prémisses consolidation et n'en pâtit pas — elle est jugée sur sa propre logique migration. value_pct=35 car la valeur EXISTE mais est entièrement en aval (conditionnelle à un rendu v4 prouvé supérieur), pas dans l'acte de migrer. build_confidence_pct=12 : à ne PAS construire maintenant ; defer jusqu'à ce que slices 2/3 livrent le lifecycle et que slice 6 produise les 3 mesures (idempotence, gain de rendu, coût/node). C'est la définition même d'une slice à différer : utile, mais prématurée et gated par dépendances dures non satisfaites."

refute.strongest_counterargument:
L'attaque la plus forte ne renverse PAS la reco "defer" — elle est correcte — mais elle révèle que l'éval est mal calibrée des DEUX côtés, et que value_pct=35 est encore TROP HAUT.

1) BLAST-RADIUS GONFLÉ SUR DE FAUX CHIFFRES (vérifié main-thread). L'éval fonde son argument-massue ("centaines de sessions de review", "589 nodes") sur des comptages fabriqués : elle annonce 589 (28 VIS+94 PIL+128 GUI+125 CPT+214 DEC). La réalité SQL est 270 nodes (5 VIS + 17 PIL + 16 GUI + 84 CPT + 148 DEC). C'est un sur-comptage de 2.2x, et CHAQUE chiffre par type est faux (94 PIL vs 17 réels, 128 GUI vs 16 réels). Le "high blast-radius" et le risque "centaines de sessions" sont donc partiellement gonflés sur des données inventées. À 270 nodes, dont seulement 122 north-star (5+17+16+84), Decisions (148) explicitement différables, la masse réelle à migrer en priorité est ~38 nodes (VIS+PIL+GUI). Ce n'est PAS un éléphant. L'éval a sapé sa propre crédibilité empirique en hallucinant la cible.

2) MAIS LA RECO TIENT QUAND MÊME, et value_pct devrait BAISSER pas monter. Même à 270 nodes, les trois gates durs vérifiés restent intacts : (a) decay_score/schema_version/content_hash = 0/1099 nodes (confirmé : aucune des 3 clés v4 n'existe ; les 40 clés metadata réelles sont 100% legacy : updated_at, priority, acceptance_criteria, tags, rationale...). Le mécanisme central de la slice (soll_validate flag debt + decay auto-resurface) est non-constructible. (b) slice 6 est un gate de mesure délibéré, confirmé dans le body du REQ lui-même ("Bloqué par REQ-AXO-901790 slice 6 pilote ±20%"). (c) La collision metadata est PIRE que l'éval ne le dit : 1097/1099 nodes ont déjà du metadata legacy peuplé (priority×560, acceptance_criteria×464). Une migration non-idempotente sur une base NEVER-delete est un risque de perte silencieuse réel — mais c'est un argument POUR defer, pas contre.

3) LA VALEUR EST PLUS FAIBLE QUE 35%, PAS PLUS FORTE. La prémisse cold-start/deferred-tools est neutre ici (la slice ne touche pas la surface MCP) — l'éval a raison. Mais le besoin de "consommabilité v4" est largement DÉJÀ COUVERT : retrieve_context_v2 (RRF FTS+vector+graph) et soll_query_context rendent déjà la prose des 270 nodes de façon exploitable AUJOURD'HUI (c'est exactement comme j'ai lu tous ces bodies). La valeur incrémentale de la migration = uniquement le delta entre "prose rendue par v2" et "structure v4 rendue par Layer B", delta NON MESURÉ (slice 6 unbuilt) et dont aucun consommateur n'existe. C'est de l'inventaire 100% spéculatif sur une feature aval. value_pct=35 sur-évalue une migration dont le seul bénéfice est conditionnel à 3 slices unbuilt ET à une mesure ±20% non encore faite. Réaliste : ~20%.

Verdict : la reco "defer" SURVIT (renforcée même : gates plus durs que décrits), mais l'éval doit être corrigée — value gonflé à 35 (réel ~20), blast-radius gonflé sur de faux comptages (270 pas 589), et build_confidence 12 légèrement trop haut vu que 0/1099 nodes portent le moindre champ v4.
refute.notes: Reco "defer" correcte et survit — mais l'éval est mal calibrée. ERREUR EMPIRIQUE MAJEURE : node counts fabriqués (éval=589 / 28+94+128+125+214 ; réel SQL=270 / 5 VIS+17 PIL+16 GUI+84 CPT+148 DEC). Sur-comptage 2.2x qui gonfle le blast-radius "centaines de sessions". Cible north-star réelle = ~38 nodes (VIS+PIL+GUI), pas un éléphant. Gates DURS confirmés et plus durs que décrits : 0/1099 nodes ont schema_version/content_hash/decay_score (vérifié — 40 clés metadata toutes legacy) ; collision metadata réelle (1097/1099 nodes déjà peuplés, priority×560/acceptance_criteria×464 — risque perte silencieuse sur base NEVER-delete) ; slice 6 gate ±20% confirmé dans le body du REQ. VALEUR SUR-ÉVALUÉE : retrieve_context_v2 + soll_query_context rendent déjà la prose des 270 nodes exploitable aujourd'hui ; le delta v4 est non-mesuré et sans consommateur → inventaire spéculatif. value_pct abaissé 35→20, confidence 12→8. Prémisse cold-start neutre (slice ne touche pas la surface MCP) : OK. GUI-PRO-104 non pertinent ici (coordination edit fichier, pas rendu de consommation). Recommandation finale inchangée : DEFER, gated par slices 2/3 (lifecycle) + slice 6 (mesure idempotence/gain-rendu/coût-par-node). Fichiers pertinents : aucun en src/ — greenfield confirmé.

value_table.recommendation: modify_then_build — extract ONLY C6 (markdown as MODE param, fully decoupled from render_hash/envelope) as a trivial ergonomic change; DEFER the rest behind Layer A. 4/9 fields (adjacent_context, rules_in_effect, decay_score, density) are INVALIDATED — substrate is Slice 2/4 greenfield. serde_json already sorts keys (no preserve_order) so RFC-8785 JCS buys near-nothing; a token-budget+truncation envelope already ships (retrieve_context_layered) so a bytes envelope adds a THIRD vocabulary on the hottest read tools. render_hash gates a contract with no current consumer and no observed breakage. defer — VERIFIED this session: 0 same-node update pairs within 60s, 0 within 5min, min gap ~301s across all RevisionChange history. The lost-update collision OCC prevents has NEVER occurred; the system is single-writer-per-node in practice. The MIL-27 parallel-expert workflow itself shards one-REQ-per-agent so it cannot self-trigger the race. The only concurrent-edit incident in corpus (REQ-AXO-328 -> GUI-PRO-104) is a SOURCE-FILE collision already owned by file-level agent-locks, a layer Slice 4 doesn't touch. Mandatory expected_revision taxes ~99.9% single-agent traffic; even opt-in OCC permanently couples the hottest write verb to a counter needing atomic CAS (no txn handle in convenience API) + sync with the separate append-only soll.revision journal (two-sources-of-truth) + the v4 content_hash (drags in Layer-A JCS). The existing append-only journal already gives post-hoc overwrite detection — the only capability a single-writer system demonstrably needs. Keep the single-call conflict-envelope spec on the shelf; build nothing until a real SOLL-node collision is observed.

experiment_then_almost_certainly_defer — the headline economic premise (cold-start tax 15-50K = 200-800 tokens x 67) is INVALIDATED for the operator's real client: in Claude Code the ~80 axon tools arrive DEFERRED via ToolSearch (confirmed this session — they were not eagerly parsed), returning <=5 schemas per intent query. The effective routing surface is ALREADY <=5, below the <=15 target, so consolidation is pre-empted in the client actually used. is_public_tool returns true for ~everything, so 67->15 is hand-authoring a 12-verb catalog + retrofitting 50+ handlers into action= discrimination + keeping all demoted names dispatchable as aliases indefinitely (the only anti-stale-binding safety) — real complexity rises while only the advertised count drops. runtime_surface.rs hard-asserts ~200 tool names -> self-inflicted test break before any LLM benefit. VERY HIGH blast on the live contract every connected LLM (incl. this evaluator) routes through. Run the cheap code-free routing A/B (eager vs deferred client) once to DOCUMENT the harness already wins; do not build for the deferred client. The premise holds only for eager-loading MCP clients (a real but secondary audience).

The TRUE value is not '2.5x compression' but 'cheap probe that will very likely REFUTE Slice 7's mass-migration premise' — reframe the experiment as gate-to-NOT-build, not gate-to-enable. Body-read tax IS real and independent of deferred-tools (GUI-PRO-102 Phase A reads each Pillar body in full at cold-start), which is why this slice survives where the tool-consolidation half dies. Run the zero-code experiment FIRST: tokenize the real baseline + hand-encode the 2 extreme nodes (PIL-AXO-9003 8152c worst-case, GUI-PRO-013 192c compact); proceed only if median compression >=2x AND a fresh LLM can derive intent from short+rules alone.

synthesis.overall_verdict: ENGAGE MIL-27 NARROWLY AND EMPIRICALLY — do NOT green-light the umbrella as a block. The milestone bundles one architecturally elegant idea (a structured v4 SOLL contract + a leaner MCP surface) on top of a knowledge base that, by every metric verified this session, does not yet exhibit the problems the experts invoke. Three load-bearing expert premises are empirically broken in the operator's actual environment: (1) the cold-start tax 15-50K = 200-800 tokens x 67 is INVALIDATED — Claude Code defers all ~80 tools via ToolSearch and surfaces <=5 per intent, so the routing surface is already below the <=15 target and consolidation (Slice 5) buys near-zero in the real client; (2) the lost-update race OCC defends (Slice 4) has NEVER occurred — 0 same-node updates within 5 minutes across all history, min gap ~301s, and the parallel-expert workflow shards one-REQ-per-agent so it cannot even self-trigger; (3) the +/-20% cold-start compression target (Slice 6) is calibrated against a fictional 15-20K baseline — the real pilot corpus is ~5.6K tokens, so the headline 2.5x gain is unreachable and the aggregate real saving is ~2K tokens against a 200K-1M context (noise). What SURVIVES and is worth pursuing: the body-read tax is real and independent of deferred-tools (GUI-PRO-102 Phase A reads every Pillar body in full at cold-start), so compressing the genuinely bloated prose nodes (e.g. PIL-AXO-9003 8152 chars) has local 3-5x value even if the aggregate is modest; the pilot-as-empirical-gate (Slice 6) is the single best piece of discipline in the milestone; and Slice 8 (self-introspection) addresses a gap the operator personally lived. THE RIGHT ENTRY POINT: run the cheap, code-free experiments FIRST (tokenize the real baseline, hand-encode 2 extreme nodes, routing A/B eager-vs-deferred) — these are hours of work and will almost certainly RE-SPEC or KILL the quantified targets before a single line of validator/envelope/OCC code is written. Build the Layer-A substrate (Slice 2) only if the dry-run proves structured short+rules lets a fresh LLM act without the prose body. Ship the markdown-MODE ergonomic (Slice 3 C6) and Slice 8 as standalone low-risk wins. DEFER Slice 4 (no observed problem), DEFER Slice 5 for the deferred-tools client (pre-empted by the harness), and DEFER Slice 7 (588-node fusion on a NEVER-delete base, zero current consumer of v4 structure). Real incremental value delivered to an LLM/client: MODEST and mostly latent — bounded by (i) compression of a handful of bloated prose nodes and (ii) consolidation value only for eager-loading MCP clients that are NOT the operator's primary surface. Risk: HIGH on the consolidation and envelope slices because they mutate the live MCP contract every connected LLM depends on (including the evaluating agent). Verdict: this is a measure-twice-cut-once milestone whose value the experts over-claimed by conflating two distinct token taxes; engage it as a sequence of cheap experiments that retire risk, not as a build-everything mandate.

cross_cutting_risks: SUBSTRATE INVERSION: Slices 3/6/7 read Layer-A metadata (short/rules/anchors/inheritance/decay_score) that does NOT exist (0/1735 nodes, jsonschema crate absent). Building any of them before Slice 2 ships placeholder/null fields — repeating the existing snapshot_id:null anti-pattern in retrieve_context_layered. Layer A (Slice 2) is the single hard dependency gating real value.

CONFLATED TOKEN-TAX ACCOUNTING: the milestone's economic case double-counts two unrelated taxes — the eager tool-schema parse tax (15-50K, NEUTRALIZED by deferred-tools in the operator's client) and the SOLL body-read tax (~5-6K, real and independent). Every quantified target (15-20K->6-8K cold-start, 2.5x compression, +/-20% gate) inherits this miscalibration; ALL numeric acceptance gates must be re-derived against the real ~5.6K body baseline before they can pass/fail informatively.

LIVE-CONTRACT BLAST RADIUS: Slices 3 and 5 mutate the MCP surface/output-shape that every connected LLM (Claude/Codex/Gemini, including the evaluating agent) routes through every Phase-A onboarding and code-diagnosis loop. A bad cutover degrades all sessions simultaneously, not one feature. Any change must ship behind a default-off flag with indefinite dispatch-alias retention — which itself dilutes the 'cleaner surface' goal.

CLIENT-CONDITIONAL VALUE: the consolidation thesis is true for eager-loading MCP clients but FALSE for Claude Code (deferred ToolSearch, <=5 schemas/intent). Treating a client-conditional benefit as universal risks spending a VERY-HIGH-risk migration budget for near-zero real-client gain. Decide the target audience (eager vs deferred clients) BEFORE building.

NEVER-DELETE FUSION HAZARD (Slice 7): metadata jsonb is already 97.9% populated with legacy keys (priority x678, acceptance_criteria x559, rationale x142). Mass migration is a non-destructive merge on a knowledge base whose policy is NEVER delete SOLL — a non-idempotent writer silently overwrites authored intent. Migration must be proven idempotent on a single node before touching 588.

SELF-MEASUREMENT GAP: every slice claims a token/routing/compression benefit, yet there is NO in-tree benchmark for routing accuracy or LLM task-success vs surface size or vs structured-vs-prose rendering. The only acceptance oracle that exists (mcp_surface_diagnostics <=15 count, retrieve_context_layered token metering) proves counts, not quality — a green gate can mask a capability regression. Build the measurement harness before trusting any acceptance gate.

COORDINATION-LAYER OVERLAP (clarified, not a conflict): GUI-PRO-104 agent-locks operate at the file-edit layer and already own the only concurrent-edit incident in corpus (REQ-AXO-328). Slice 4 MVCC operates at the SOLL-node layer where 0 collisions have ever occurred. They are orthogonal — but this means Slice 4's only empirical justification lives at a layer it does not touch, weakening rather than supporting the build case.
